skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python scripts through the inference.sh belt CLI in a hosted sandbox with common data, web, media, and document libraries.
skill.summary: Run Python code in a hosted sandbox with common data and media libraries.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command and network patterns. Most backtick hits are Markdown formatting or CLI examples, but the skill intentionally allows arbitrary Python execution through the belt CLI and supports outbound HTTP calls. No malicious intent, credential theft, prompt injection, or hidden exfiltration was found in SKILL.md, so this is publishable with a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Python Execution Through External CLI
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill grants Bash access to belt and shows belt app run commands that submit caller-provided Python code to infsh/python-executor. This is intended functionality, but it can execute untrusted code and process sensitive data outside the local workspace. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The allowed-tools entry and command examples directly show external command execution with user supplied code.

medium_findings.title: Networked Sandbox and HTTP-Capable Code
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill depends on inference.sh and includes examples that use requests to fetch external websites and APIs. This is legitimate for scraping and API tasks, but users could send private inputs to remote services or fetch untrusted content. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The dependency on inference.sh and explicit HTTP examples confirm network use, but no hidden endpoint or malicious exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Many reported Ruby or shell backtick issues are Markdown inline code, fenced examples, package names, and documentation text rather than executable Ruby backticks. The weak cryptography and network reconnaissance alerts also appear to be keyword matches in descriptive prose, not harmful code. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: SKILL.md is documentation only, and the cited lines contain Markdown, CLI examples, or plain text without embedded malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allows belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash(belt *), which gives the assistant permission to run belt commands. This is necessary for the skill, but marketplace users should understand that code and inputs are sent to an external executor. Confidence: 0.92. Confidence reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and directly enables the external command path.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Code Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented input schema accepts a code field, and examples pass Python snippets to the executor. This pattern is acceptable for a code execution skill but should be treated as high impact when prompts include secrets or untrusted code. Confidence: 0.89. Confidence reasoning: The schema and examples clearly show arbitrary code payloads, while the surrounding documentation frames the behavior as intended.

content.user_title: Run Python Tasks in a Remote Sandbox
content.value_statement: Agents often need Python execution without setting up local packages or runtime state. This skill routes Python scripts to a hosted inference.sh sandbox with common libraries for data, web, media, and document tasks.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python code through the inference.sh belt CLI.
Uses common libraries such as pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, requests, BeautifulSoup, Pillow, MoviePy, and trimesh.
Supports web scraping, API calls, data processing, image processing, video generation, 3D processing, and PDF generation.
Returns files saved under the outputs directory.
Offers default and high-memory execution variants.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated session.
Runs in a CPU-only hosted environment without GPU or ML-specific acceleration.
Execution timeout is limited to the documented range of 1 to 300 seconds.
Do not send secrets, private datasets, or regulated data unless the external service is approved for that use.

use_cases.title: Analyze Small Business Data
use_cases.description: Load tabular data, calculate summaries, and generate charts without configuring a local Python environment.

use_cases.title: Prototype Web Data Collection
use_cases.description: Test scraping logic or API calls with requests, BeautifulSoup, Selenium, or Playwright in a disposable runtime.

use_cases.title: Generate Media and Documents
use_cases.description: Create images, videos, PDFs, or 3D files from short Python scripts and return the generated outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints the installed pandas version and a simple calculation result.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to build a small table of monthly sales, create a bar chart, save it to outputs, and summarize the result.

prompt_templates.title: Fetch and Parse a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to request a public web page, parse its title and first headings, and explain any network or parsing errors.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Asset Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to process a dataset, create a chart and a PDF report in outputs, and return the generated artifacts with a concise summary.

output_examples.input: Analyze a small sales table and create a chart.
output: A concise summary of total and average sales.
A generated chart file returned from the outputs directory.
Notes about assumptions used in the analysis.
A generated model file returned from the outputs directory.
A short message with vertex count and export status.

output_examples.input: Fetch public API data and summarize it.
output: A readable summary of selected fields from the response.
A warning if the request fails, times out, or returns unexpected data.

best_practices: Review code before execution, especially when it came from an untrusted prompt or web page.
Keep inputs small and remove secrets before sending data to the remote sandbox.
Save generated artifacts under outputs so the executor can return them.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for private credentials, customer records, or regulated data without approval.
Do not run destructive, abusive, or unauthorized scraping workloads.
Do not assume local files or local network resources are available in the hosted sandbox.

faq.question: Does this run Python locally?
faq.answer: No. It sends code through the inference.sh belt CLI to a hosted sandbox.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it access the internet?
faq.answer: The examples include HTTP requests, so users should treat network access as available and potentially sensitive.

faq.question: Can it return generated files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files saved under outputs are documented as automatically returned.

faq.question: Is it safe to run untrusted code?
faq.answer: Use caution. The code runs in a sandbox, but it can still consume resources or expose data sent to it.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it intentionally executes arbitrary Python through an external network service.
